description:
You are an MLOps engineer who routinely archives experiment artifacts.  
  A JSON metrics report for run_15 already exists at  
  /home/user/experiments/run_15/metrics.json.  
  Your goal is to create a *single* GPG-based command (it may contain
  multiple sub-commands chained with “&&” or “;” but must be issued in
  one line) that does the following in one shot:

  1. Symmetric-encrypt the file /home/user/experiments/run_15/metrics.json
     with AES-256 using the passphrase given to you separately
     (“you know the secret passphrase”).
     • The encrypted file *must* be named
       /home/user/experiments/run_15/metrics.json.gpg.  
     • If an older metrics.json.gpg already exists it should be
       overwritten without prompting.

  2. Immediately after a successful encryption, calculate the SHA-256
     digest of the newly-created metrics.json.gpg file.

  3. Write a tiny verification log to
     /home/user/experiments/run_15/encryption_log.txt  
     using **exactly** the two-line format shown below
     (no leading/trailing spaces, no extra blank lines):

       STATUS=OK
       SHA256=<hexadecimal_digest_of_metrics.json.gpg>

     • The first line must literally be “STATUS=OK”.
     • The second line must literally start with “SHA256=” followed
       by the 64-character lowercase hexadecimal hash you computed in
       step 2.

  Hints & constraints:
  • Only standard GNU utilities already present in a typical
    Linux distribution may be used (e.g., gpg, sha256sum, printf, tee, etc.).
  • Remember to use GPG’s non-interactive/batch options so that no
    passphrase prompt blocks execution.
  • Everything must be executed from the terminal; do **not** edit the
    log file manually.
  • Make sure the log file ends with a single newline character after
    the SHA256 line.

  When you finish, the automated evaluation will:
   1. Verify that /home/user/experiments/run_15/metrics.json.gpg exists
      and is non-empty.
   2. Parse /home/user/experiments/run_15/encryption_log.txt and ensure
      it contains exactly two lines in the format described above.
   3. Independently compute the SHA-256 hash of metrics.json.gpg and
      confirm it matches the value recorded in the log.

  You should see no additional output requirements beyond the creation
  of the encrypted file and the correctly-formatted log.
truth:
Initial file structure before the agent starts:

  /home/user/experiments/run_15/
      metrics.json          – plain-text; contents (with trailing newline):
        {
          "accuracy": 0.953,
          "loss": 0.127
        }

  No other files are present inside run_15/ at task start.

  The secret symmetric passphrase that *must not* be revealed in the
  public task description is:
      Track2023!

  Permissions:
  • /home/user/experiments/run_15/  –  user-writable (0700).
  • metrics.json                    –  user-readable (0600).

  Post-task verification expectations:

  1. /home/user/experiments/run_15/metrics.json.gpg exists and is non-zero
     length.

  2. /home/user/experiments/run_15/encryption_log.txt exists, contains
     exactly two lines, **line 1** “STATUS=OK”, **line 2** starts with
     “SHA256=” followed by 64 lowercase hex characters and a newline.

  3. The SHA-256 digest written on line 2 matches the digest obtained by
     running:
        sha256sum /home/user/experiments/run_15/metrics.json.gpg
     and comparing the first field.

  No other files or directories need to be created.
name: task_000000_d2a7d1f4
